"We now generalize the above results by showing that equation (1.1) in real line with mul-
tiplicative space-time white noise has an unique invariant measure when f is linear growth
and g is square growth. This result can be applied to the stochastic burgers equation and
reaction-diffusion equation. We prove that the existence and uniqueness of solution for e-
quation (1.1) in weighted L2- space based on Gy¨ ongy.[7]. We prove that the solution with
an expectation value is uniformly bounded in time by providing a certain dissipativity to f
and g and applying the mild Itˆ o formula developed by Da Prato, Jentzen, and R¨ ocker in [10].
Combined with some compactness theories and the Krylov-Bogoliouboff theorem, we prove
that the family of laws L(u(t)) is tight in weighted L2- space, then we obtain that the exis-
tence of invariant measure for (1.1)."

"We now generalize the results presented above by demonstrating that equation (1.1) on the real line, with multiplicative space-time white noise, possesses a unique invariant measure when the growth rate of function f is linear and the growth rate of function g is quadratic. This result has practical implications for both the stochastic Burgers equation and the reaction-diffusion equation. To establish the existence and uniqueness of a solution for equation (1.1) in the weighted L2-space, we rely on the work of Gy¨ ongy [7]. We also prove that the solution, with an expected value, remains uniformly bounded over time by introducing a specific dissipativity condition to functions f and g, and employing the mild Itˆo formula developed by Da Prato, Jentzen, and R¨ ocker [10]. By utilizing various compactness theories and the Krylov-Bogoliouboff theorem, we further demonstrate that the family of probability measures L(u(t)) is tight in the weighted L2-space. Consequently, we establish the existence of an invariant measure for equation (1.1)."

S(t)φ = G(t,·) ∗ φ and G(t,x) =

Example 2.1. The functions ρ(x) = (1 + |x|2)−1/2and ρ(x) = exp(−2|x|) are admissible

Our estimation about the solution of (1.1) is stated as follows,based on Istv´ an Gy¨ ongy and

Denoting ∆ as the Laplace operator in the unbounded domain R and S as the semigroup generated by ∆ on L2(R) or on some extensions of L2(R), for all t > 0 and all φ ∈ L2(R), we can express S(t)φ = G(t,·) ∗ φ and G(t,x) = 1/√(4πt)exp(−|x − y|2/4t), where ∗ denotes the convolution operator. In the subsequent paragraphs, we introduce weighted L2-spaces and define an admissible weight ρ as a bounded, continuous, and positive function in the intersection of L1(R) and L∞(R). This admissible weight ρ satisfies the condition G(t,·) ∗ ρ ≤ C(T)ρ for all t ∈ [0,T], where C(T) is a positive constant depending on T. We define H = L2ρ(R) as the set of functions ω : R → R such that the integral R|ω(x)|2ρ(x)dx is finite, and kωk2H is defined as the integral R|ω(x)|2ρ(x)dx over R. Two examples of admissible weights include ρ(x) = (1 + |x|2)−1/2 and ρ(x) = exp(−2|x|). Based on the work of István Gyöngy and David Nualart [7], we present our estimation regarding the solution to equation (1.1) in a weighted space.

Below is a paragraph from an academic paper.
Polish the writing to meet the academic style, improve the spelling, grammar, clarity, concisio and overall readability. When necessary, rewrite the whole sentence. Furthermore, list all modifigstign and explain the reasons to do so in markdown table.
"Based on Gy¨ ongy and Nualart[7], the proof of this result uses standard tech-
niques. It can be divided into the following steps:
(i). we prove the existence and uniqueness of local solution by taking the ball B(0,N) of
radius N in H , applying fixed-point theorem, we can conclude that for ∀N, there exists a
unique solution in B(0,N).
(ii). we need to obtain the necessary estimates for the global existence. Take the auxiliary
function v, such that u = v + w, where w is an Itˆ o integral and bounded, by the variational
method we can deduce that v is bounded. Thus u is bounded for t ∈ [0,T]. Then we can
obtain the results.
For a detailed proof we can refer to Gy¨ ongy and Nualart ."

Based on the work of Gyöngy and Nualart [7], the proof of this result employs standard techniques and can be divided into the following steps:

(i) Firstly, we establish the existence and uniqueness of a local solution by considering the ball B(0,N) with radius N in H. By applying the fixed-point theorem, we can conclude that for any given N, there exists a unique solution within B(0,N).

(ii) Next, we aim to obtain the necessary estimates for global existence. To achieve this, we introduce an auxiliary function v such that u = v + w, where w represents an Itô integral and is bounded. Using the variational method, we can deduce that v is bounded. Consequently, u remains bounded for t ∈ [0,T], allowing us to obtain the desired results.

For a more detailed proof, please refer to the work of Gyöngy and Nualart [7].

Theorem 2.11: Under the assumptions (A1), (A2), and (A3), and given u0 in H ∩ L2 ρ(R), where ρ is an admissible weight, let (Pt)t≥0 represent the Feller semigroup in H associated with the solutions u(t, ·, u0) of Equation (1.1). If there exists another admissible weight ˆ ρ satisfying the condition (2.19):

∫ρ(x)(ˆ ρ(x))^(−1) dx < ∞,

and if u(t, ·, u0) is bounded in probability in L2 ˆ ρ(R), i.e., for every ε > 0, there exists an R > 0 such that for all t > 0, P(‖u(t, ·, u0)‖^2 ˆ ρ ≥ R) < ε, then there exists an invariant measure for the Feller semigroup (Pt)t≥0 on H. Furthermore, if the solution of equation (1.1) exhibits a contraction property as defined in Definition (2.5), then the invariant measure is unique, thus rendering (Pt)t≥0 ergodic.

Remark 2.12: Examples of such admissible weights ρ and ˆ ρ include ρ = (1 + |x|^2)^(-2) and ˆ ρ = (1 + |x|^2)^(-1). It can be easily verified that these weights satisfy the inequality (2.19).

In order to fulfill the boundedness in probability requirement stated in Theorem 2.11, the dissipativity condition is necessary. We will now present a sufficient condition to satisfy this condition.

Consider the sequence of functions defined as follows: fn = [(f ∧ n) ∨ (−n)ϕn(x)] ∗ εn and gn = [(g ∧ n) ∨ (−n)ϕn(x)] ∗ εn. Here, ϕn(x) represents a smooth function that satisfies ϕn(x) = 1 if |x| ≤ n, and ϕn(x) = 0 if |x| ≥ n + 1. Additionally, εn is a function with support included in the interval [-1/n, 1/n]. It is worth noting that fn and gn have compact support in x, are uniformly bounded, globally Lipschitz in u, and satisfy the same Lipschitz conditions and growth rate as f and g, with constants independent of n. Let un(t,x) denote the solution to (1.1) with the coefficients fn, gn, and initial condition u0(x). It can be observed that for each t ∈ R, we have:

lim
n→+∞ un(t,x) = u(t,x)

Furthermore, this convergence is uniform. Thus, we obtain:

lim
x→+∞ u(t) = lim

x→+∞ un(t,x) = 0

Similarly, similar results can be obtained for equation (3.12).

this will be described below.
Lemma 3.4. Let ∆ be the Laplace operator on H and denote σ(∆) the spectrum of ∆, then
σ(∆) = (−∞,0"

Our subsequent proof relies on the spectral properties of the Laplace operator in a weighted space, which will be explained in the following section.

Lemma 3.4: Let ∆ denote the Laplace operator on H, and let σ(∆) represent the spectrum of ∆. It can be observed that σ(∆) = (−∞,0).
